nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, heavily vegetated surface waters; blackwater streams, flooded forest margins, and the edges of larger Amazonian rivers with tannin-stained, soft, acidic conditions

diet.notes: An insectivore in the wild, feeding primarily on small insects and invertebrates fallen at or near the water surface. In captivity tends to accept small floating flakes, freeze-dried foods, and live or frozen fare such as Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, Moina, and grindal worm. The upward-facing mouth means food that sinks is generally ignored; surface feeding is strongly preferred. Occasional small live insects such as young crickets are accepted and beneficial.

goodWith: small peaceful characids (cardinal tetra, neon tetra, ember tetra)
avoidWith: aggressive or fast-swimming fish that outcompete at the surface

summary: The Marbled Hatchetfish is a small, peaceful surface dweller from Amazonian blackwater streams, reaching around 3 to 4 cm. It is closely related to the Common Hatchetfish but notably smaller. Groups of six or more are recommended. A tight-fitting lid is essential, as this species is an active jumper.
careNotes: The Marbled Hatchetfish is the smaller of the two commonly kept hatchetfishes; it reaches roughly 3.0–4.0 cm compared to the Common Hatchetfish (Gasteropelecus sternicla), which grows to around 4–6 cm. See also [common-hatchetfish]. Both species share the same deeply keeled, hatchet-shaped body adapted for surface-cruising and gliding jumps. The escape risk for this species is high — it has been reported to launch itself through surprisingly narrow gaps, and a tightly fitting lid with all openings covered (including filter and heater slots) is generally considered essential. A long tank footprint is broadly advised over a tall format to provide adequate surface swimming space for the school. A minimum group of 6 is widely recommended; groups of 10 or more tend to display more relaxed, natural behavior. This species is considered timid and may struggle in community tanks with boisterous or fast-moving companions that outcompete it for surface food. Wild specimens are caught from tannin-rich Amazonian blackwater; soft, slightly acidic conditions with some surface cover are generally preferred. Leaf litter and driftwood help replicate the natural biotope. Gentle water movement is broadly advised — strong surface flow can prevent floating food from being accessible. Water quality maintenance is important; wild-caught specimens have been documented to be prone to stress-related illness on import, and a proper quarantine period with proactive treatment is commonly recommended. Commercial specimens are almost entirely wild-caught.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding in home aquaria has not been reliably documented and commercial specimens are wild-caught. Eggs have been documented to hatch after approximately 36 hours at 25°C under laboratory conditions, but no established captive breeding protocol exists for hobbyists. Wild spawning is associated with vegetated surface habitats typical of the rainy season.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Page covers both Common and Marbled Hatchetfish; Marbled-specific data extracted. Tank size 20 gal (76 L), temperature 24–27°C, school 6–12, intermediate care level, very high escape risk noted.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked high-stakes fields. Adult size 30–35 mm (3.0–3.5 cm), temperature 20–28°C, pH 4.0–7.0, hardness 18–179 ppm (~1–10 dH), native range: Amazon (Brazil, Peru, Bolivia, Colombia), Orinoco (Venezuela), coastal drainages (Guyana, Suriname). Breeding unrecorded in captivity; all specimens wild-caught. Recommends 75 cm minimum footprint and group of 10+.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked: max 3.5 cm SL, temperature 24–28°C, pH 5.0–8.0, hardness 5–19 dH, native range (lower/middle/upper Amazon, Caqueta River Colombia). Family Gasteropelecidae, order Characiformes confirmed. Eggs hatch ~36 hours at 25°C. Minimum aquarium 80 cm. FishkeepingWorld (secondary 4) returned 404; Practical Fishkeeping (secondary 3) returned 403. 3-source minimum met via Aquarium Co-Op + SeriouslyFish + FishBase.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding protocol documented for home aquaria. Wild spawning associated with surface vegetation during rainy season. Commercial specimens are wild-caught.
